items.title: L’Algérie enquête sur l’origine d’une vague d’incendies inédite
items.description: Six morts selon un premier bilan officiel, une « véritable catastrophe » évitée mardi à Seraidi sur les hauteurs d’Annaba, des habitants et des malades évacués, des milliers d’hectares réduits en cendre : depuis le début de l’été, l’Algérie fait face à une vague d’incendies d’une ampleur inédite de par son étendue géographique. Les sapeurs-pompiers sont engagés dans une bataille sans fin contre les feux de forêt . La canicule et les vents violents qui soufflent sur certaines régions rendent difficile leur tâche. À Bejaia, des températures de 55° C ont été relevées sur les lieux des incendies. A chaque fois qu’un incendie est éteint après des efforts colossaux, d’autres se déclenchent, ce qui met à rude épreuve le dispositif national de lutte anti-incendie. L’éparpillement des incendies sur une trentaine de wilayas du nord complique davantage la situation et ne permet pas aux unités de la Protection civile de prêter main forte aux régions les plus touchées. La multiplication des feux de forêt pose la question de leur origine, criminelle ou non. En visite mardi soir à Annaba où un incendie d’une grande ampleur a obligé les autorités à évacuer d’urgence des malades de l’hôpital de réduction fonctionnelle de Seraidi et des habitants de cette ville perchée sur les hauteurs du massif de l'Edough à 850 mètres d'altitude, le ministre de l’Intérieur et des Transports, Saïd Sayoud, a annoncé deux mesures phares. Algérie : 1.555 incendies enregistrés depuis le 8 juillet La première est l’engagement de l’Etat à indemniser tous les sinistrés des incendies. Rien qu’à Seraidi, 150 habitations ont été endommagées par les flammes mardi, selon le ministre. La seconde annonce faite par Saïd Sayoud est la « poursuite des enquêtes sécuritaires pour déterminer les causes de ces incendies », a-t-il dit. « Nous avons enregistré 160 incendies depuis la fin du week-end dernier au niveau des wilayas du nord. Les enquêtes des services de sécurité se poursuivent pour déterminer si ces incendies sont d’origine criminelle ou naturelle », a déclaré Saïd Sayoud. Dans la journée de mardi, la Protection civile a enregistré 170 incendies dans 19 wilayas du pays. Les pompiers ont réussi à éteindre 104 alors que 66 sont toujours en activité, selon la même source. Et depuis le 8 juillet, 1555 feux de forêt ont été enregistrés en Algérie, selon la Protection civile. L’article L'Algérie enquête sur l'origine d'une vague d'incendies inédite est apparu en premier sur TSA .

items.title: Iran’s post-war gamble on the young
items.description: Tehran is red and black when I arrive. Millions of people in black mourning attire carried red flags, the stark emblem of blood and martyrdom, seeking revenge for their leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was assassinated on February 28. “We Must Rise,” declare the banners. War songs ricochet off the walls in streets punctuated by elegies on the martyrdom of Imam Husayn and his Companions at Karbala. This is collective grief channelled as state resilience. An elderly supporter I meet, Akbar Ali Jafari, puts it like this: “The war has given the Revolution a new lease on life.” The founding purpose is resurrected and its leadership cast as embattled defenders of the nation rather than an ageing, contested regime. A vehicle carrying coffins of Iran’s late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his family members, makes its way as people attend a funeral procession for Khamenei. — Reuters In early July, a towering fist was constructed a few days before the funeral proceedings of the slain leader. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) chose Revolution Square as the spot to assert that the Islamic Republic stood undefeated. What unfolded during the funeral proceedings was a grand spectacle of unity and continuity orchestrated as much for domestic consolidation as for Western consumption. A statue symbolising the hand of Iran’s late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at Enghelab Square in Tehran, Iran. — Reuters The national mood changes The day after the state funeral in July, the scene changed. Art galleries around Enghelab Square reopened, women in t-shirts and jeans sketched with their heads uncovered. In the affluent Tajrish or centuries-old Grand Bazaar, boys and girls sped by on motorbikes or hung around vaping. In Pasdaran, cafes filled with couples holding hands or groups of youngsters talking over each other loudly. I thought of what Mr Jafari had told me earlier, that the Islamic Republic wants to bring the younger generation into its fold. And indeed, I was looking at the evidence in the public sphere. Restrictions on dress codes appeared to have been further relaxed along with those that regulated music and mixed gatherings outdoors. This was a different Iran from the one just four years ago, managed by a morality police or Gasht-e-Ershad Guidance Patrol force set up during the Ahmadinejad presidency in 2005 to guard the nation’s chastity. But the force was suspended after Mahsa Amini’s death in custody was met with nationwide protests in 2022. Women ripped off their headscarves as they rallied to the cry, “Women, Life, Freedom” in the face of a crackdown. “Those were horrible days,” recalls 24-year-old activist Maryam Rezaei, who works as a manager at a private business. “We used encrypted WhatsApp and Telegram messages to coordinate protests. My friends were detained by the notorious Guidance Patrol and sent to re-education centres. Now there are no white vans with dark green stripes chasing you in public places.” Iranian women walk past a banner with a picture of the former Chief of Staff of the Iranian Armed Forces, Mohammad Bagheri, in Tehran, Iran, July 20, 2026. — Reuters Anti- and pro-government Iranians have a profound connection to their culture and see Iran as a civilizational state, rooted in thousands of years of history. Its complex layers include the Shia Islamic identity, which merges with Persian heritage as opposed to supplanting it. Pre-Islamic heritage is preserved through festivals such as Nowruz, the Iranian New Year, celebrated to welcome spring, along with the winter solstice or Yalda Night and the fire festival Chaharshanbeh Suri. Ferdowsi, the legendary 11th-century poet’s work, Shahnameh , Republicis regarded as a cornerstone of nationhood. So embedded is this pride that the clergy, despite its ideological leanings, has learned to accommodate it and even use it to strengthen nationalist sentiment. The US-Israel war on Iran strengthened the expression of this identity. I speak to Mohammad Mehdi, a young man who runs a handicraft shop with his brother, to gain a sense of the mood. He and his friends heard Trump declare that their civilization should be “erased” and watched America and Israel bomb centuries-old monuments in his hometown of Isfahan, known as Nesf-e-Jahan or ‘Half the World’ for its art, culture, and beauty. And so when there were threats to blow up bridges and power grids, Mehdi and his friends would go to symbolically defend them. “Iran isn’t Venezuela,” he asserts. “We will fight back if they attack us again. We will defeat them first, and then we can fight our internal battles.“ A billboard depicting U.S. President Donald Trump laid in a coffin on a building in Tehran, Iran. — Reuters Women like Maryam and her friend Fatemeh Ahmadi are the digitally native generation which powered anti-regime demonstrations earlier this year. In a paradoxical realignment, however, they are the very same demographic that has now rallied around the state and flag since the US-Israel war on Iran broke out. “How could we support the invading forces?” says Fatemeh. “We are a proud nation, and we back the Islamic Republic for standing firm against the aggression of the US and Israel.” These young Iranians see the government as safeguarding their history and civilisation and are responding to calls for national solidarity, survival instinct, and civilizational pride. Social media was flooded with young people taking pride in Iran’s resilience and defiance in the face of war, with the ‘Lego videos’ by Explosive Media going viral. Faced with an existential external threat, the anger of ‘People vs the Regime’ was supplanted by the hostility of ‘the Aggressor vs Iran’. People management In the backdrop of the war, the softening of social strictures could be a sign of strategic recalibration. How will the government manage the youth, who have demonstrated nationalist loyalty during war, but whose social and cultural values are fundamentally at odds with the theocratic system? At a roadside cafe, this is being discussed by history buff Ali Reza, who is not sure whether the pattern has staying power. “It’s too early to predict whether this change is temporary or will get entrenched,” he says. “They might allow young people to dress with more freedom, listen to their music, and mingle in public, as long as they do not organise politically.” Such a strategy would, in effect, separate lifestyle freedom from political freedom and trade the visibility of social control for the stability of political control. But people close to official circles point out unspoken fears about infiltration, spurred further by US attempts at fomenting uprisings in the wake of the attacks. It is conceivable, though speculative, that the government will relax moral codes for public spaces, but ramp up digital and physical surveillance to detect dissent and espionage. People walk in the Grand Bazaar in Tehran, Iran. — Reuters Beyond the question of lifestyle freedoms lies a deeper dividing line between the revolutionary guards and the clergy who emerged together from the 1979 revolution. The clergy provided ideological direction and the guards the muscle. By the eve of this war, however, the Revolutionary Guards had consolidated its role as the regime’s economic, military, security and regional backbone. The clergy, by contrast, was confronted with its waning authority as it lost ground to the youth in the culture wars. Shielding the people from the US attacks has made the IRGC the guarantor of national survival and repositioned it as the centre of gravity. It has experienced this ascendancy in the past during an earlier national security crisis. After the Iran-Iraq war, the force’s prestige, resources and autonomy massively expanded. Historically, the IRGC has treated social control less as a doctrinal mission and more as an operational necessity, and any opposition as a security vulnerability. How the clergy responds to easing of social controls could be a potential point of friction. It may cause disquiet within the clerical establishment, between the pragmatists who accept the relaxation as a safety valve, and the conservatives who may see it as a surrender and doctrinal retreat. If the internal distribution of political capital and power changes in the post-war period, the IRGC will hold the decisive veto. Follow the money Young people point out, however, that lifestyle freedoms are not their only or greatest concern. Social change alone cannot placate any demographic group in Iran, including the 60per cent youth, because the economy eclipses it. “Life isn’t easy, with soaring inflation strangling low-income families,” says Fatemeh. “Even the middle class is feeling the heat. With an uncertain future, some even feel they cannot breathe freely.” Many young Iranians say the economy is hollowing the country out. Inflation hovers at 80pc, the highest since World War II, and unemployment is a palpable fear. They highlight the need to find an exit from global isolation and sanctions. “No family can survive on a single person’s earnings. My father works as a transporter. I study but work part-time, my sister also earns, only then we can afford a living in Tehran,” says 21-year-old Ali Vahidi, an engineering student. “Daily life is so tough, how then do I dream of a future?” The hardline Revolutionary Guards, however, seem to believe time is on their side. They appear convinced that a prolonged war with “untrustworthy enemies” could yield further geo-strategic advantages: weakening the US security umbrella in the Gulf, providing a safety valve for allies such as the Houthis in Yemen and Hezbollah in Lebanon, and cementing Iran’s emergence as a regional power. They believe Iran as ‘victorious’ and control of the Strait of Hormuz can provide them a renewed economic lifeline. The narrow sea passage, which controls one-fifth of global oil and energy supplies, is a double-edged sword; if it strangles the world economy, then it will have rebound effects on Iran’s economy as well. After a week in the city, as I head back to Tehran’s Imam Khomeini Airport, the changing images of Iran’s history are assembled into a photomosaic-in-process, where the dominant is still not clear. The seeds of a new social contract may have been planted, but the terms are still being written.

items.title: When Chairman Wontumi’s 20-year sentence marks a turning point in the fight against political impunity
items.description: There is an old legal truth that deserves renewed attention : “Justice delayed is not justice denied where the law permits prosecution, but justice obstructed by power is a betrayal of the Republic.” Chairman Wontumi’s conviction is therefore not merely about one individual. It is an indictment of a culture that believed political influence could permanently silence the law. For years, Ghana watched forests disappear, rivers turn brown, communities flood, and livelihoods vanish beneath the greed of illegal mining. Petitions after petitions were reportedly submitted. Warnings were allegedly issued. Yet decisive enforcement appeared conspicuously absent. The question is not whether government officials knew illegal mining existed. The more uncomfortable question is what they did with that knowledge. If petitions concerning Akonta Mining reached the highest levels of the previous administration, then history will judge not only those accused of illegal mining but also those who chose hesitation over constitutional duty. A government that preaches the rule of law while allowing influential allies to enjoy what many perceived as political immunity weakens public confidence in justice itself. Former President Nana Akufo-Addo and former Vice President Dr. Mahamudu Bawumia cannot simply escape legitimate public scrutiny over allegations that repeated concerns about illegal mining involving politically exposed persons failed to receive timely and decisive action. Hence the nickname “ Mr clearing agent” . Leadership carries responsibility not only for acts committed but also for opportunities ignored. The High Court has now demonstrated something profoundly important: no political office, party title or financial influence should place anyone above the law. As for the critics of the decision, their arguments speak for themselves, and the less said about them, the better. It is obvious the court’s ruling is based on the evidence and the law, not politics. After reading the judgment several times, I am convinced by the judge’s reasoning, especially the circumstantial evidence and the cross-examination of the defence witness. In my view, attempts to politicise the case and the opposition’s press conference fail to address the legal issues in the judgment. Justice Audrey Kocuvie-Tay deserves commendation for discharging her constitutional mandate without apparent regard for the political stature of the accused. Delivering judgment against an ordinary citizen requires legal competence. Delivering judgment against a politically influential figure demands something more than judicial courage. Courts exist precisely for moments when power expects exemption. Predictably, the conviction has generated accusations of selective justice. Equality before the law does not require every suspect to be prosecuted simultaneously. Criminal investigations mature at different times, evidence is gathered at different speeds, and prosecutions proceed according to available proof. The constitutional guarantee is equal application of the law, not synchronised arrests. However, those defending the judgment must also embrace the same principle consistently. If investigations reportedly exist concerning persons associated with the current administration or any other political party, they must proceed with equal determination. Justice cannot become a revolving door that prosecutes yesterday’s favourites while protecting today’s allies. The law must remain gloriously indifferent to party colours. Illegal mining is not an NPP crime or an NDC crime. It is a crime against Ghana. Every poisoned river denies children safe drinking water. Every destroyed forest mortgages the country’s environmental future. Samreboi community pays the price for someone’s unlawful profit. Nature conducts no political registration before delivering disaster. Floodwaters do not ask whether victims voted NPP, NDC or any other party. Environmental destruction is ruthlessly non-partisan. Some people in the NPP seem determined to create more disaffection for their own party than their political opponents ever could. How on earth do you tell Ghanaians that if you return to power, Chairman Wontumi will simply be set free? Then others chime in with the familiar “the tables will turn” refrain, as though justice is a football match with home and away fixtures. Such utterances are not only childish but very damaging to their party. If you genuinely believe there are legal grounds, then support an appeal and seek a reduction of the sentence through the courts. That is how the rule of law works. How do you expect the average Ghanaian to willingly vote for a message that appears to put political power above due process? In the end, the electorate will decide. We wish you all the best. But the hard truth is whatever the outcome on appeal, one lesson should already be unmistakably clear: political influence has an expiry date; accountability does not. The true measure of this moment will not be whether one prominent individual was convicted. It will be whether Ghana finally develops the institutional courage to pursue every alleged illegal mining kingpin, regardless of wealth, office, surname or political affiliation. Until then, prosecutions risk appearing episodic rather than transformative. The Republic cannot afford selective outrage or selective justice. It needs selective fear by every public official, financier and politically connected individual contemplating environmental crime. Fear that one day the Constitution, the courts and the evidence will meet. At the very least, it is a matter of public record that President Mahama’s first administration prosecuted some of its own officials. There was even a deputy minister who expressed a personal wish and was subsequently dismissed from office. For too long, Ghana’s forests have carried the scars of human greed while politics supplied convenient insurance policies. If this judgment marks the cancellation of those policies, then history may remember it not simply as the conviction of one man, but as the day the law finally found its voice and reminded the powerful that the Republic belongs not to politicians, but to justice.

items.title: US death toll in Iran conflict increases to 18 — AP

items.title: La société civile d’Aru interpelle le gouverneur sur les incursions d’hommes armés depuis le Soudan du sud
items.description: Les incursions répétées d’hommes armés en provenance du Soudan du Sud inquiètent de plus en plus les populations vivant le long de la frontière dans le territoire d’Aru, en Ituri. Face à cette menace transfrontalière grandissante, la société civile et les notables locaux alertent également sur une montée de la criminalité dans les principaux centres commerciaux de la région.

items.title: DJ Chicken hails Seyi Tinubu as ‘Daddy’ after release from prison
items.description: Popular Nigerian content creator Ademola Abiodun, also known as DJ Chicken, has stirred reactions on social media after describing Seyi Tinubu, the son of President Bola Tinubu, as ‘daddy’ shortly after regaining his freedom. The entertainer, who was released from the Kirikiri Correctional Centre on Tuesday, July 21, 2026, also shared a photo of Seyi Tinubu on his Instagram Stories with the caption: “Hmm ddddy.” He has also changed his Instagram display picture to one of Seyi. DJ Chicken was released on Tuesday after spending nearly two weeks in prison. Announcing his freedom in a video on social media, the content creator who was seen with his lawyer said “I’m back oo, freedom at last.” “God bless the Nigerian government, God bless Seyi Tinubu, and our president. I love you guys. It is not easy, my fans. People that are free don’t know what God has done for them. Freedom is good”, he said. He was arraigned on a three-count charge bordering on alleged violations of the Cybercrimes Act, breach of peace and making alleged threats to kill Seyi Tinubu. The charges stemmed from a viral video in which DJ Chicken allegedly claimed that President Bola Tinubu would not win re-election in 2027 and was accused of making threats against the President’s son. Following widespread criticism over the video, the content creator withdrew the remarks, explaining that they were intended as a joke to gain attention and increase his online popularity. Despite the apology, he was arrested and brought before the court. Vanguard News The post DJ Chicken hails Seyi Tinubu as ‘Daddy’ after release from prison appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: PFIPC scandal: Apologise and resign now, Atiku tackles Tinubu
items.description: …Cites conflicting CBN, OAGF accounts By Omeiza Ajayi, Abuja Former Vice President Atiku Abubakar has told President Bola Tinubu to apologise to Nigerians and resign over what he described as a cover-up surrounding the Presidential Foreign Investment Promotion Council PFIPC accounts scandal. His demand followed conflicting testimonies by the Central Bank of Nigeria CBN and the Office of the Accountant-General of the Federation OAGF. In a statement on Wednesday by his Senior Special Assistant on Public Communication, Phrank Shaibu, the Presidential Candidate of the African Democratic Congress ADC said the CBN’s testimony before the House of Representatives fundamentally contradicts the narrative earlier advanced by the OAGF, plunging the Tinubu administration into one of the gravest credibility crises in Nigeria’s democratic history. He insisted that the president owed Nigerians an apology and had lost the moral authority to remain in office if he failed to act. “President Tinubu owes Nigerians an unreserved apology for presiding over this unprecedented collapse of public accountability. If he cannot immediately restore public confidence through a genuinely independent investigation and ensure that everyone responsible is held to account, then he has forfeited the moral authority to continue in office. The honourable course is simple: apologise to Nigerians and resign,” he declared. The former Vice President said he could only sympathise with President Tinubu, adding that no serious leader needed a clairvoyant to know that his administration was trapped in a web of deception. “When two of your most sensitive financial and monetary authorities publicly contradict each other on the same transaction, no serious leader needs a clairvoyant to know that his administration is trapped in a web of deception and a profound crisis of credibility. The President cannot continue to watch from the sidelines while his government tears itself apart with conflicting narratives,” he said. Atiku insisted that Tinubu must clarify which of the two accounts he believed, warning that his continued silence deepened public suspicion. “If he believes neither, why has he not acted? Every day he delays ordering a truly independent investigation, he deepens public suspicion that preserving his administration’s image has taken precedence over establishing the truth,” he said. He also faulted the attorney-general of the Federation for maintaining silence on the matter, describing it as a compounding factor in the crisis. He called on the National Assembly to immediately summon the Accountant-General of the Federation and the Governor of the Central Bank to appear together before its investigative committee under oath so that Nigerians could finally hear one truthful account of the transaction. “The PFIPC scandal has ceased to be merely about disputed accounts. It has become a referendum on President Tinubu’s integrity and competence. If a single individual could allegedly orchestrate a scheme that has thrown two of Nigeria’s most critical financial and monetary authorities into public contradiction, Nigerians are entitled to ask how many other abuses may have escaped detection because of weak oversight and institutional failure,” he said. The post PFIPC scandal: Apologise and resign now, Atiku tackles Tinubu appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Contrôle des finances publiques : renforcement des compétences des magistrats financiers
items.description: La capitale sénégalaise accueille, depuis hier, 21 juillet 2026, le premier séminaire professionnel de la Commission de renforcement de capacités de l’Organisation des institutions supérieures de contrôle ayant des compétences juridictionnelles (Jurisai). Cette rencontre de trois jours est placée sur le thème de la gestion des compétences des professionnels des fonctions juridictionnelles. « Gestion des compétences du professionnel des fonctions juridictionnelles : défis et perspectives ». Tel est le thème du premier séminaire professionnel organisé par la Cour des Comptes du Sénégal, en collaboration avec l’Organisation des institutions supérieures de contrôle des finances publiques à fonctions juridictionnelles (Jurisai). La rencontre, ouverte à Dakar, et retransmise en vidéo-conférence, se poursuit jusqu'au 23 juillet 2026. Elle réunit des chefs d'Institutions supérieures de contrôle (Isc), des procureurs généraux, ainsi que de nombreux magistrats du siège et du ministère public. Selon le Premier président de la Cour des comptes, Abdoul Madjib Guèye, cet évènement s’inscrit « dans le prolongement direct du mandat confié à la juridiction sénégalaise pour présider la Commission de renforcement de capacités » mise en place par l’Assemblée générale constitutive de Jurisai. Il a souligné que « les fonctions juridictionnelles exercées par nos institutions ne sont pas des attributions ordinaires : elles touchent directement aux droits, à la situation patrimoniale et, parfois, à la carrière des justiciables de nos juridictions financières ». M. Guèye a rappelé que la légitimité d’une Isc reposait avant tout sur « des femmes et des hommes compétents, agissant selon des processus de qualité au sein d’une institution résolument professionnalisée ». D’après le Premier président de la Cour des comptes, malgré l’existence de normes internationales, la situation sur le terrain demeure contrastée d'un pays à l'autre. Il souligne plusieurs défis persistants au sein des Isc : une formalisation encore incomplète des cadres de compétences, des programmes de développement professionnel parfois déconnectés des réalités du terrain, ainsi que des mécanismes d’évaluation des performances à consolider. Face à ces constats, Emmanuelle Borrel, représentante de Jurisai, soutient que la gestion des compétences constitue aujourd'hui « un véritable levier stratégique de gouvernance ». Elle estime d'ailleurs que ce séminaire doit servir de base référentielle à l'ensemble des Isc dotées de fonctions juridictionnelles, afin qu'elles puissent mener à bien l'intégralité de leurs missions, qu'elles soient juridictionnelles ou non. Fatou SY

items.title: Europol : la menace terroriste s’amplifie et change d’algorithme
items.description: « Le terrorisme en Europe entre dans une nouvelle phase », previent Europol dans son rapport annuel publie en juillet. En 2025, dix etats membres ont signale 45 attaques : 22 commises, 20 dejouees et trois ayant echoue. Parallelement, 486 suspects ont ete interpelles dans 21 pays, contre 449 en 2024. Des djihadistes aux communautes de l'ultraviolence La permanence du danger djihadiste reste la premiere conclusion. Le djihadisme est en cause dans 24 des 45 attaques recensees et 347 arrestations, soit plus de 71 % du total. Ses attentats ont cause cinq des six morts et 81 blesses. Le « nouveau visage », decrit par Europol, ne remplace donc pas les menaces anciennes : il s'y ajoute, les brouille et complique leur detection, alors que quelques mois plus tot, Europol interpellait sur le risque de voir se developper un terrorisme robotique . Le changement tient d'abord au profil des personnes interpellees. Leur âge moyen etait de 27 ans, 130 avaient 18 ans ou moins et le plus jeune seulement 12 ans. Europol observe chez certains jeunes radicalises une connaissance superficielle des doctrines, remplacee par un melange instable de djihadisme, neonazisme, misogynie, nihilisme, ressentiments personnels ou fascination pour les tueries de masse. Terrorisme dans l'UE : Europol dresse le portrait d'une menace de plus en plus fragmentée Europol a publié cette semaine son rapport annuel TE-SAT 2026 (European Union Terrorism Situation and Trend Report), qui dresse le bilan de la menace terroriste dans l'Union européenne pour… pic.twitter.com/4ZhRXb2fXw — FT Osint (@FTOsint) July 16, 2026 Les plateformes accelerent cette recomposition. Reseaux sociaux, messageries securisees, forums, services decentralises et univers lies aux jeux video permettent de recruter, de diffuser des modes operatoires et de passer plus rapidement de la consommation d'images a la violence reelle. La « gamification » : classements, defis et recompenses, contribuerait a banaliser les contenus extremes et a effacer la frontiere entre activite numerique et passage a l'acte. Le rapport s'attarde notamment sur le « Com network », ecosysteme mouvant associant cyberattaques, extorsion sexuelle de mineures et violences physiques, jusqu'aux incendies, agressions au couteau ou meurtres. Aucune ideologie unique ne domine cet univers : le statut dans le groupe et la recherche de reconnaissance peuvent suffire a mobiliser certains participants. Sur LinkedIn, Europol resume cette mutation par une formule : la violence devient un instrument «d'identite, de reconnaissance et d'appartenance». Pour les services antiterroristes, surveiller les organisations structurees ne suffit plus lorsque la menace naît aussi d'individus isoles, naviguant d'une doctrine a l'autre au gre des algorithmes.

items.title: ADRA Ghana donates relief items, calls for sustainable recovery support for June 29 flood victims
items.description: The Adventist Development and Relief Agency (ADRA) Ghana has called for a shift from emergency relief to long-term recovery support for families affected by the June 29 floods, saying many victims now face the challenge of rebuilding their homes and livelihoods. The organisation appealed while presenting relief items to the National Disaster Management Organisation (NADMO) in Accra to support ongoing assistance for flood victims. Citing NADMO data, ADRA Ghana said nearly 90,000 households were affected by the floods, leaving many families displaced after losing homes, personal belongings and sources of income. Speaking at the presentation, ADRA Ghana Country Director, Dr Isaac Kankam-Boadu, said although emergency relief remains critical, sustainable recovery should focus on helping affected families regain their livelihoods. “Our mandate calls us to action, and that is why we have mobilised resources from our international office, our regional office and within the Seventh-day Adventist Church in Ghana to support victims of the floods,” he said. Dr Kankam-Boadu disclosed that ADRA Ghana had mobilised about US$20,000, equivalent to approximately GH¢224,000, to procure relief items, including toiletries, student mattresses, detergents, washing powder and other household essentials identified by NADMO as urgent needs. He said the organisation intends to work beyond relief distribution by partnering with NADMO and development partners to help affected families rebuild their livelihoods. “After this presentation, we will engage our donor partners and, once NADMO identifies the beneficiaries, work collaboratively to provide skills training and livelihood support so affected victims can generate income and bounce back to their former state,” he said. He added that ADRA Ghana is also prepared to deploy volunteers to provide psychosocial support and health education during community-level relief distribution. Dr Kankam-Boadu attributed the growing severity of flooding in Ghana to the combined effects of climate change and human activities. “Climate change is leading to higher rainfall, while the rapid concretisation of our urban areas reduces the ability of the soil to absorb excess water. At the same time, citizens must be educated on how to manage the environment to reduce flood risks,” he stated. Receiving the items on behalf of the government, NADMO Deputy Director-General in charge of Technical, Alhaji Ahmed-Rufai Afarahim, expressed appreciation to ADRA Ghana and assured that the relief items would reach the affected communities. “We promise these items will get to the victims of the June 29 flood disaster,” he said. He described the donation as a demonstration of national solidarity, noting that disasters affect people regardless of their religion or background. “The disaster doesn’t know who is a Christian and who is a Muslim. We thank ADRA for what they have done,” he added. The June 29 floods affected several communities across Ghana, causing widespread destruction and displacing thousands of households. As recovery efforts continue, ADRA Ghana says sustained partnerships and livelihood support will be critical to helping affected families rebuild their lives.

items.title: GRA Customs explains how imported vehicles are classified and valued for duty assessment
items.description: Officials of the Ghana Revenue Authority (GRA) Customs Division have explained the process used to classify and value imported vehicles, stating that duty assessments are based on internationally accepted customs procedures designed to ensure fairness and consistency. Speaking on Joy FM’s Super Morning Show , Principal Revenue Officer at the Safe Bond Car Terminal in Tema, Emelia Ewenam Ankrah, and Senior Revenue Officer at the Technical Services Bureau, Daniel Kwame Ntem, said vehicle duty assessment follows two key stages — classification and valuation. According to the officials, Customs first classifies a vehicle to determine the applicable tax rate before establishing its customs value for the calculation of duties and taxes. Ms Ankrah explained that once an importer or declarant submits an application for duty assessment, Customs officers identify the vehicle using its unique Vehicle Identification Number (VIN), commonly referred to as the chassis number. She said the VIN is decoded to retrieve key technical information, including the vehicle’s fuel type, whether petrol, diesel, electric or hybrid, the year of manufacture, seating capacity and other specifications. These details, she noted, enable Customs to assign the appropriate Harmonized System (HS) Code , which determines the tariff classification and corresponding import duty. The officials explained that certain vehicle characteristics can significantly affect classification. For instance, buses are classified differently from passenger vehicles because of their seating capacity and intended purpose. They stressed that accurate VIN decoding is critical to ensuring every imported vehicle is placed under the correct tariff heading. Once the classification process is completed, Customs proceeds to the valuation stage, where the customs value of the vehicle is determined before the applicable duties and taxes are calculated. The officials said classification and valuation are separate but complementary processes that help ensure transparency, consistency and compliance with international customs standards. Their explanation comes amid heightened public interest in Ghana’s vehicle import duty regime, particularly following discussions on customs valuation methods and the application of the Home Delivery Value (HDV) system. The GRA Customs Division maintained that the established procedures are intended to ensure that all imported vehicles are assessed fairly and in accordance with the law.

items.title: Under soaring pressure after 'cockroach' movement protests, India's education minister promises reforms, debate
items.description: India’s education minister said that Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s administration was committed to addressing the concerns of protesting youth and exam reforms, in the latest sign that the government was beginning to respond to growing protests. The comments by Dharmendra Pradhan are his first since the youth-led “cockroach” movement launched a protest in New Delhi last month seeking his resignation over the leak of medical school test papers that affected some two million students and was linked to several student suicides. The protests by the self-named Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) — which began as an online satire — involved a few hundred young people when they were first launched. This week, the movement has grown into the biggest youth challenge to Modi in his third term as the prime minister as tens of thousands of protesters took to the streets of Delhi on Monday, clashing with police who used tear gas and batons to push them back. The protests gained further momentum on Tuesday when the main opposition Congress party, led by Rahul Gandhi, launched a sit-in outside the gates leading to Modi’s residence. Gandhi, his sister Priyanka Gandhi Vadra and several other senior opposition leaders were detained by police after they refused to end their protest, but were released late on Tuesday. “Our government remains 100 per cent committed to discussing NEET and addressing every genuine concern of our youth on the floor of the house,” Pradhan said in a post on X late on Tuesday, referring to the medical school entrance test. “The students of India deserve far better than being treated as props in a political campaign,” he said, referring to Gandhi’s protest. “We owe our students more than outrage. We owe them answers, reforms and accountability. That is exactly what our government remains committed to delivering,” he added. Pradhan’s comments follow those by Modi on Tuesday — his first on the issue — who vowed to punish those behind the exam leaks and also called on his lawmakers to help create a foolproof exam system. Separately, Health Minister JP Nadda held talks with CJP leaders on Monday for the first time and assured them the government would consider their demands. Gandhi demands debate in Parliament Youth anger and protests over the exams have become the biggest challenge for the prime minister in his third term. The CJP’s surge in popularity reflects frustrations among young Indians over issues such as job shortages, as well as exam leaks, which they say happen frequently. It has also given opposition parties a rare opening to pressure Modi and his Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), who count young voters as a core base and have won a series of state elections since a shock result in the 2024 election , when Modi did not win an outright parliamentary majority. “Why is the Indian education system collapsing? Why are papers leaking? Why is education so expensive in India? Why do families have to destroy themselves financially to educate their children?” Gandhi said in a video message recorded while he was in detention at a Delhi stadium on Tuesday night. “These are legitimate questions. And there’s absolutely nothing wrong with asking these questions,” he said. Gandhi said the students’ issues should be debated in parliament on Wednesday and Modi should apologise to the students for the police action on Monday and begin reforms to the examination and education systems as soon as possible. The CJP movement gained momentum from the support of activist Sonam Wangchuk, who began a hunger strike on June 28, but was forcibly moved to a hospital by police on Saturday. On Tuesday, he was moved to a private hospital following a court order in response to a petition from his wife that alleged he had been illegally detained.

items.title: Full List: 6 requirements to access FG’s loan to buy phone, laptop
items.description: The Federal Government has begun accepting applications for its Credit for Laptops, Internet, Connectivity and Knowledge Digital Devices (C.L.I.C.K.D.) Programme, giving eligible Nigerians the opportunity to obtain smartphones, laptops and tablets through consumer credit. The initiative is being implemented by the Nigerian Consumer Credit Corporation (CREDICORP) in partnership with the Federal Ministry of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy. It is designed to improve access to digital devices while encouraging the use of locally assembled products. For the current pilot phase, applicants must satisfy several requirements before they can be considered for the scheme. 1. State of residence is required Applicants must indicate the state where they currently live by selecting from Nigeria’s 36 states or the Federal Capital Territory (FCT). The information will be used to profile applicants and manage the programme nationwide. 2. Employment details must be provided The application requires applicants to state their current employment status. The available categories include: Salary earner Freelancer or self-employed Business owner Student Job seeker National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) member Applicants may also disclose their monthly income, although this section is optional. Income brackets range from below N100,000 to more than N1 million. 3. Applicants must be part of an eligible digital skills programme The first phase of the initiative is currently limited to participants in the Federal Government’s Three Million Technical Talent (3MTT) programme. However, the application portal also lists several other digital training programmes, including: Andela Learning Community Learn2Earn ALX HNG Internship AltSchool Africa Genesys Tech Hub Their inclusion suggests the programme could be expanded to participants from these organisations in future phases. 4. Applicants must choose a device and explain its intended use Those applying must indicate whether they are requesting a smartphone, laptop or tablet. They are also required to explain how the device will be used. The available options include: Learning and professional certifications Remote work Freelancing Software development Business expansion Content creation School and other educational purposes Applicants can select multiple reasons where applicable. 5. Applicants must agree to verification Before submitting the application, applicants must confirm that the information they have supplied is accurate. They must also consent to being contacted about their application and authorise CREDICORP and its partners to verify the submitted information to determine their eligibility for the credit facility. 6. Applicants must indicate how they heard about the programme As part of the application process, applicants are required to state how they became aware of the C.L.I.C.K.D. Programme. The available options include: 3MTT Social media Friend or family School Employer Google Search Other This information forms part of the application details that must be completed before submission. How to apply Visit the website. Go to credicorp.ng/clickd, the official C.L.I.C.K.D. landing page. Click “I’m Interested.” This takes you to the application form. Vanguard News The post Full List: 6 requirements to access FG’s loan to buy phone, laptop appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: MP Elikplim Akurugu commissions renovated KG block at Taifa Community School
items.description: The Member of Parliament for Dome-Kwabenya, Faustina Elikplim Akurugu, has commissioned a renovated kindergarten block at the Taifa Community School, describing education as the foundation for the constituency’s development. The renovation of the kindergarten block was sponsored by Imperial Homes through its Imperial Charity Foundation as part of efforts to provide pupils with a safe and conducive environment for teaching and learning. The commissioning ceremony was attended by the Ga East Municipal Chief Executive, Edmund Agboh; the Assembly Member for Taifa; the Queenmother of Taifa; members of the Council of Elders; Unit Committee Members; the Municipal Director of Education; the Chairman of the Conference of Heads of Schools; the School Management Committee (SMC); the PTA Chairman and executives; headteachers, teachers, pupils and residents. Speaking at the event, Madam Akurugu thanked Imperial Homes and the Imperial Charity Foundation for partnering with her office to improve educational infrastructure in the constituency. “I want to express my sincere appreciation to Imperial Homes and the Imperial Charity Foundation for supporting this important project. “By investing in this kindergarten block, you are investing in the future of our children and the future of Dome-Kwabenya,” she said. The MP said education remains one of the key priorities of her tenure and highlighted several interventions she has undertaken to improve access to quality education in the constituency. According to her, these include the distribution of desks to schools, exercise books to pupils, mathematical sets to BECE candidates, renovation of the Kwabenya Senior High School library, distribution of school uniforms, financial assistance to needy students and the introduction of the Elikplim Scholarship Scheme. She noted that creating a conducive learning environment would help improve academic performance and inspire children to reach their full potential. The Ga East Municipal Chief Executive, Edmund Agboh, urged teachers, pupils and the community to ensure the facility is properly maintained. “This facility is for the benefit of our children. Let us all take responsibility for protecting it and ensuring that it serves generations of pupils,” he said. The Municipal Director of Education, Eric Sey, commended the MP for prioritising education and assured that the renovated facility would be put to good use. “We are grateful to the Member of Parliament for this intervention. The Education Directorate will ensure that this facility is well maintained and used to support effective teaching and learning,” he stated. School authorities also welcomed the intervention. Headteacher Madam Dorcas described the renovation as timely, saying it would provide a better environment for early childhood education. Another headteacher, Moris Andrew Quaye, expressed appreciation to the MP and Imperial Homes, noting that the project would positively impact teaching and learning. Headmaster Mr. Godfred Tenkorang also praised the MP for what he described as her unwavering commitment to education, saying the renovated kindergarten block adds to the numerous educational projects she has implemented across the constituency. The renovated facility is expected to improve learning conditions for kindergarten pupils at Taifa Community School while supporting efforts to strengthen basic education in the Dome-Kwabenya Constituency.

items.title: Dar arrives in Manila to attend Asean Regional Forum ministerial meeting
items.description: Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar arrived in Manila on Wednesday to participate in the 33rd Asean Regional Forum (ARF) ministerial meeting, the Foreign Office (FO) said. “A busy programme of engagements lies ahead, including participation in the ARF plenary meeting and bilateral meetings with counterparts on the sidelines,” the FO said in a post on the social media platform X. It said that upon his arrival, Dar was received by Elmer G Cato, the assistant secretary at the Philippines’ foreign affairs department, Pakistan’s Ambassador to the Philippines Asima Rabbani and other officials. In a statement issued on Tuesday night, the FO said that the high-level meeting will bring together foreign ministers and delegates from 27 ARF member states, adding that the Asean secretary general will also attend the meeting. It said that Dar will participate in discussions on “political and security issues affecting the Asia-Pacific region, with a special focus on promoting peace, stability, and mutual cooperation through sustained dialogue and diplomacy”. He will also hold bilateral meetings with his counterpart from the Philippines and several other participating dignitaries on the sidelines of the meeting, it said. “Established in 1994, the ARF has remained a pivotal platform for multilateral consultations on regional security issues. Since becoming a member in 2004, Pakistan has maintained an active role within the forum, consistently offering constructive contributions to its regional programmes and objectives,” the FO statement concluded. Dar meets with Russian, Bangladesh FMs On the sidelines of the Asean meeting, Dar met with Bangladesh FM Khalilur Rehman, who is also the president-elect of the 81st session of the United Nations General Assembly, the FO said. The ministers “reviewed the positive trajectory of Pakistan–Bangladesh relations and the renewed impetus by the deputy prime minister’s visit to Bangladesh last year”, the statement said, adding they reaffirmed their “shared commitment to further strengthening bilateral ties”. The FO further said that the two dignitaries welcomed growing engagement between their countries, including “enhanced people-to-people contacts and improved connectivity following the commencement of direct flights this year”. They agreed to sustain the “positive trajectory”, the statement added. Dar and Rehman also exchanged views on “regional and international issues of mutual interest”, the FO said. In January, direct flights between Pakistan and Bangladesh commenced after a 14-year hiatus as an inaugural flight from Dhaka landed at Karachi Airport. Ahead of the Asean moot, Dar met with Russian FM Sergey Lavrov in Manila. The two leaders reviewed bilateral relations between their countries and reaffirmed their commitment to “further strengthening cooperation in trade, energy, connectivity, and other key areas, as well as enhancing coordination at multilateral forums”, the FO said. “They underscored the importance of sustained high-level engagement in advancing the Pakistan-Russia partnership,” it added. The two foreign ministers also exchanged views on key regional and international developments and agreed to remain in close contact.

items.title: What Is Wrong With Us: We cannot always choose our circumstances. We can always choose our response
items.description: Africa has endured colonialism, conflict and extraction. It possesses minerals, land and the world’s youngest population. And still the question persists, quietly and without mercy: what are we choosing to do with what we have? At Some Point, The Explanation Becomes The Prison Perhaps no continent has endured more genuine historical adversity than Africa. Colonialism disrupted societies, arbitrarily drawn borders fragmented communities, slavery robbed millions of their dignity and global inequalities continue to distort trade and development. These facts must never be minimised or forgotten. And yet. There is another truth, far less comfortable to acknowledge, that sits beside those facts and refuses to leave. History may explain where we started. It cannot permanently excuse where we choose to remain. Across Africa, it has become common to attribute almost every contemporary challenge to circumstances beyond our control. When institutions fail, we blame history. When infrastructure deteriorates, we blame previous governments. When corruption flourishes, we blame politics. When cities flood, we blame climate change. Many of these explanations contain truth. But explanations are not solutions. And at some point, which few of us are brave enough to name precisely, explanations become excuses. Circumstances become hiding places for choices we simply refuse to make. That is the question this article will not look away from. What is wrong with us? “Circumstances introduce us to life. Choices introduce us to history.” NyansaKasa — Words of Wisdom Sit with that for a moment. We rarely control how the story begins. But we are never without agency in how it continues. Every society that has ever transformed itself understood, at some point, that waiting for circumstances to improve was itself a choice, and one of the most expensive a nation can make. The question is not what happened to us. It is what we are doing, right now, with what remains. When Circumstances Become Convenient Excuses No nation chooses whether rain falls. But every nation chooses whether drains are maintained. No country chooses global commodity prices. But every country chooses whether to diversify its economy. No government chooses where minerals are located. But every government chooses how transparently those resources are managed. No community chooses every disease outbreak. But every community chooses whether refuse is dumped into gutters or whether sanitation becomes a daily non-negotiable. The distinction matters because too often Africa confuses unavoidable circumstances with entirely avoidable decisions. When plastic waste blocks drainage systems, rainfall is not the primary problem. When roads deteriorate because overloaded vehicles repeatedly destroy them, weather is not the principal issue. When schools fall into disrepair despite substantial investments in their construction, time is not the enemy. Neglect is. Choice is. Circumstances present challenges. Choices determine outcomes. That sentence deserves to be read again slowly, and then applied to every institution, every community and every individual on this continent. “History rarely rewards those who waited for perfect circumstances. It remembers those who created better ones.” NyansaKasa — Words of Wisdom Japan rose from the ruins of a world war. Singapore built a global economy from a city with almost nothing beneath its soil. South Korea was once poorer than many African nations and chose, in the space of two generations, to become a technological powerhouse. Rwanda emerged from the darkest hour a people can endure and chose, deliberately and consistently, to rebuild. None of them had perfect circumstances. They had something rarer and more powerful: the collective decision to stop waiting for permission from history to move forward. Africa’s Greatest Resource Is Not Beneath the Ground Africa holds approximately 30% of the world’s known mineral reserves. It has vast arable land, immense renewable energy potential and the youngest population of any continent on earth. By 2050, Africa’s population will approach 2.5 billion people, one of the greatest concentrations of human potential anywhere in human history. And yet natural wealth has never, in the whole of recorded history, guaranteed national prosperity. Because resources are circumstances. Institutions are choices. Minerals do not govern themselves. Rivers do not educate children. Sunlight does not eliminate corruption. Population growth alone creates not prosperity but pressure, unless it is met by the deliberate choices of governance, education, investment and accountability that convert human potential into lasting human progress. ‘Resources are circumstances. Institutions are choices.’ “Natural resources may enrich a nation temporarily. Wise choices enrich generations.” NyansaKasa — Words of Wisdom Botswana has no ocean. It has no strategic port. It sits landlocked in southern Africa, blessed with diamonds and governed, crucially, with a seriousness of purpose that most resource-rich nations have failed to sustain. Mauritius has even fewer natural endowments and has nonetheless built one of Africa’s strongest and most diversified economies through precisely that quality: the consistent, unglamorous, deeply disciplined choice to govern well when it would have been so much easier not to. The Cost of Choosing Convenience Over Responsibility Many of Africa’s greatest challenges are not the product of insufficient knowledge. They are the product of insufficient discipline. We know that dumping refuse into gutters contributes to flooding, yet many continue. We know that routine maintenance costs far less than replacing destroyed infrastructure, yet maintenance budgets are routinely sacrificed. We know that corruption diverts scarce public resources from schools, hospitals and roads, yet many continue to participate in it whenever it appears personally convenient. None of these outcomes result primarily from ignorance. They result from choices. And that is the most uncomfortable truth of all, because it means the solution does not lie elsewhere. It lies with us. Africa’s children are watching. They are learning not only from speeches but from behaviour. They observe how we respond to adversity, how we treat public property, how seriously we take our responsibilities as citizens. One day they will inherit our institutions. More importantly, they will inherit our habits. And habits, unlike policies, do not change because an election was held. “Wisdom is not measured by what we know. It is measured by what we consistently choose to do.” NyansaKasa — Words of Wisdom That distinction should stop every one of us cold. We have attended the conferences. We have written the reports. We have delivered eloquent speeches about transformation and the Africa we want. And then, in the car on the way home, some of us have thrown plastic out of the window. Knowledge tells us what is right. Wisdom is what happens when we choose it anyway, even when no one is watching, even when it costs something. The Choice That Still Belongs To Us Africa’s story is not finished. It is not predetermined. It is not imprisoned by colonial history, economic inequality or global competition, however real and however painful those realities remain. The next chapter will be determined not by what was done to Africa but by what Africa chooses to do next. Every road properly maintained instead of neglected. Every river protected instead of poisoned. Every young person inspired instead of abandoned. Every act of honesty chosen over corruption. Every citizen who decides that integrity matters even when no one is watching. These choices may appear small in isolation. Together, they are the only force in human history that has ever genuinely changed a nation. History will record that Africa possessed minerals, land, brilliant young people, extraordinary resilience and immense potential. But history will ask one question that no continent can escape: what choices did we make with the gifts we were given? Circumstances may explain where we are today. They do not determine where we must be tomorrow. That decision, still, belongs to us. “Your circumstances may influence your journey, but your choices determine your destination.” NyansaKasa — Words of Wisdom Before we blame history, geography, politics or fate, perhaps we owe ourselves one moment of genuine reflection. Not the comfortable kind, performed in public, in eloquent language, to an admiring audience. The uncomfortable kind. The private kind. The kind that asks, without flinching, what we have personally chosen today that made this continent even slightly better than it was yesterday. And if the answer is nothing, then perhaps that is precisely what is wrong with us. And perhaps, more importantly, it is exactly where the healing begins. ABOUT THE AUTHOR Ing. Professor Douglas Boateng is a pioneering international industrial engineer, governance strategist and Pan-African thought leader whose work continues to shape boardroom thinking, supply chain transformation and industrialisation across the continent and globally. Africa’s first Professor Extraordinaire in Supply Chain Management, he is an International Chartered Director and Chartered Engineer, a recipient of numerous lifetime achievement awards, and the author of several authoritative books. He is also the founder and scriber of NyansaKasa (Words of Wisdom), whose daily reflections on leadership, accountability and purposeful living have reached one million readers worldwide. His driving conviction: Africa’s transformation is not waiting for better rhetoric. It is waiting for better choices, made by leaders willing to serve something larger than themselves.

items.title: ‘I’ll take you down without a second thought’ – Burkina Faso’s Traore warns challengers
items.description: Abidjan, Ivory Coast Ibrahim Traore, the head of Burkina Faso’s military junta, sought last week to stamp out speculation of a power struggle at the highest levels of government, warning he intended to remain the west African country’s only leader. The prolonged absence of two influential junta figures has fuelled rumours of a tussle within the military government, which came to power in a coup in September 2022. “There can’t be two captains on one ship,” the authoritarian Traore told community leaders in Ouahigouya, northern Burkina, on Thursday. “Everyone who works with me knows how I operate,” the 38-year-old captain continued. “If anyone screws up, I’ll take you down without a second thought. There are no strongmen around me,” he threatened. The speculation of infighting focuses on the junta’s second- and third-in-command — Oumarou Yabre, one of the most influential figures in Burkina’s security apparatus, and Farouk Azaria Sorgho, junta spokesman and commander of a powerful Rapid Intervention Battalion. Yabre — who heads the intelligence services and National Security Council, and is considered to be Burkina’s second-in-command — has not been seen in public since late May. A number of observers reported apparent tensions between Traore and Yabre as far back as 2023. These were swiftly and publicly denied by both. A security official, requesting anonymity, told AFP Yabre had stopped going to his office at the National Intelligence Agency and the security detail assigned to his home has been cut substantially. “He didn’t appear at the traditional flag-raising ceremony at the presidency, which he usually attends, or at the recent ceremony at the presidential palace to promote three senior officers to general,” another security official noted. – ‘Abducted’ – Yabre’s entourage says several of his close associates have been abducted or arrested, including Islamic preacher Ishaq Kindo, who had criticised a bill regulating religious freedoms. “This man is fundamentally bad,” Traore said of Kindo on Thursday. “He thought he was powerful because he created an Islamic police force in 2019.” Two officials close to Yabre were removed from their posts by presidential decree on June 17 — one the CEO of the National Hydrocarbons Company, the other a ministerial advisor for youth affairs. Sorgho, the regime’s high-profile third-in-command, has not appeared publicly since mid-May. He commands Rapid Intervention Battalion No 4, stationed in Ouagadougou, and is spokesman for KORAG, a body Traore set up to define and implement the junta’s “strategic vision”. As such, he has become central to cleaning up public administration, denouncing cases of financial misconduct and meting out harsh punishments. Since mid-May, Battalion No 4 has been run by Sorgho’s deputy and security officials allege the commander has been “abducted” by Traore associates. His father, Adama Luc Sorgho, the junta’s infrastructure minister until last year, was arrested and then “abducted” by armed men, his entourage says. Security operations have intensified in the capital over the past two months. Roads that had been closed for years have been reopened so traffic can be rerouted away from the presidential palace, where security checkpoints have been set up, local residents reported. The post ‘I’ll take you down without a second thought’ – Burkina Faso’s Traore warns challengers appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Malaysian tycoon Syed Mokhtar urged to revoke appointment of port chairman linked to Epstein
items.description: KUALA LUMPUR/SINGAPORE, July 22 (Reuters) - Civil society groups have called on Malaysian billionaire tycoon Syed Mokhtar Al-Bukhary to revoke the appointment of Sultan Ahmed Bin Sulayem as executive chairman of logistics giant MMC Port Holdings due to his alleged ties to the late Jeffrey Epstein. Reuters reported exclusively last week that Emirati Bin Sulayem was set to take control of Malaysia's largest port operator, five months after his resignation from Dubai's DP World amid scrutiny over his email exchanges with financier Epstein, a convicted sex offender who died in 2019.

items.title: Tehran's security services did not mistreat French diplomats, Iranian ministry official quoted
items.description: DUBAI, July 22 (Reuters) - Two French diplomats at the centre of a dispute between France and Iran were not ill-treated by Iranian security personnel, according to an Iranian foreign ministry official quoted by state media on Wednesday. France has said that the diplomats in Tehran had their phones taken, were forbidden to contact the embassy and verbally intimidated, breaking international diplomatic conventions. One of the officials, the cultural attache, was hit. The French foreign ministry on Tuesday summoned the Iranian charge d'affaires to protest the incident.

items.description: Le maire de New York, Zohran Mamdani, a déclaré mardi soir que son administration n'a pas la capacité juridique d'arrêter le Premier ministre israélien Benjamin Netanyahu, comme il l'envisageait dans le cadre du mandat d'arrêt délivré par la Cour pénale internationale à l'encontre du dirigeant israélien. Il a appelé le gouvernement fédéral à le faire si ce dernier venait à l'Assemblée générale de l'ONU en septembre.

items.title: Una testigo revela que la terapia de los Andic incluía una situación extrema en un barranco
items.description: El caso Andic ha dado un nuevo giro de tuerca con la declaración de una testigo que compromete la posición de Jonathan Andic, investigado como presunto autor de la muerte de su padre, Isak Andic, durante una excursión a la montaña de Montserrat (Barcelona) el 14 de diciembre de 2024. La testigo, intérprete británica y colaboradora ocasional de la terapeuta de los Andic, Julia Lüderwaldt, ha contado a los Mossos d’Esquadra que la terapia familiar contemplaba, entre otras cosas, que padre e hijo vivieran una experiencia extrema en un barranco , según ha avanzado El Periódico y han confirmado fuentes del caso. Hasta ahora, lo que había trascendido es que Isak y Jonathan habían acudido, solos, a hacer la excursión para hablar de sus problemas. Seguir leyendo
